coredata.dc:title: Dual-modular immunosensor for bongkrekic acid detection using specific monoclonal antibody
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Xue-Ming
Li, Li-Hua
Liang, Hong-Zhi
Li, Jia-Dong
Chen, Zi-Jian
Luo, Lin
Lu, Yi-Na
Zhong, Yu-Xin
Shen, Yu-Dong
Lei, Hong-Tao
Wang, Hong
Xu, Zhen-Lin
coredata.dc:description: Bongkrekic acid (BA) is a mitochondrial toxin that causes high mortality but is often mistakenly categorized as other food poisonings. The immunoassay of BA is still challenging since the specific antibody is unavailable. In this work, a monoclonal antibody specific to BA was first generated and a dual-modular immunosensor for on-site and laboratory detection was established. The antibody showed good affinity (Kd=0.33 μM) and sensitivity (IC50 =17.9 ng/mL in ELISA) with negligible cross-reactivity with common mycotoxins. In dual-modular conditions, fluorescence assay (FA) was conducted based on the inner filter effect of carbon dots (CDs) and oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), while the colorimetric assay (CA) was conducted using TMB2+-mediated rapid surface etching of gold nanostars (Au NSs). The proposed immunosensor showed good sensitivity and reproducibility to BA in food samples, with a limit of detection lower than 10 ng/mL and recovery ranging from 80.0% to 103.6%, which was in good consistence with that of standard LC-MS/MS. Overall, the proposed immunosensor is an ideal tool for screening BA contaminants in food with good sensitivity and high effectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bongkrekic acid
Monoclonal antibody
Dual-modular immunosensor
Fluorescence assay
Colorimetric assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the starting Au NSs
Fabrication of dual-modular immunosensor for BA detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China ( 32061160473 ), the Science and Technology Project of Dengta Laboratory , China ( DT20220033 ), and the Science and Technology Project of Shantou Custom , China ( 2022STK001 ).

This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (32061160473), the Science and Technology Project of Dengta Laboratory, China (DT20220033), and the Science and Technology Project of Shantou Custom, China (2022STK001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of mAb against BA. a) Purity identification of mAbs; M: marker, 1: reducing gel, 2: non-reducing gel; b) Sensitivity of mAbs using ic-ELISA; c) The exothermic spectra of the mAb versus BA from ITC; d) The fitted curves and affinity constants of mAbs versus BA.

Characterization of CDs. a) The TEM images of CDs, Inset image: a1) The HRTEM of CDs; a2) Particle-size distribution of CDs from Nano Measurer; b) The XRD pattern of CDs; c) Emission and excitation spectra of CDs, inset is a typical image of CDs solution under UV–vis light; d) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs in the absence and presence of oxTMB; e) The Fourier transform infrared spectrometer of CDs; f) Full spectrum XPS of CDs. Narrow scan survey spectra of CDs; g) C1s peak spectrum; h) N 1 s peak spectrum); i) O 1 s peak spectrum.

TMB2+ induced etching of Au NSs. a)-c) TEM images of as-prepared Au NSs at different magnifications after etching. d) Absorbance spectra of different reagents on the Au NSs oxidation; The volume of Au NSs is 100 μL and the final volumes of the above solutions are 200 μL. e) UV–vis absorption spectra and the color were of 50 μL AuNSs solution after the addition of different amounts of BA standard solutions.
Optimization of the process of etching. a) The concentration of H2O2. b) The concentration of TMB. c)The concentration of CTAB.

The performance of dual-modular immunosensor. a) Plotting of CDs fluorescence intensity versus BA concentrations. b) Plotting of ∆λ versus BA concentrations. c) Photographs of the immunosensor at different concentrations of BA (0–100 ng/mL). d) Relationship between the dual-modal immunosensor and LC-MS/MS for BA detection in fluorescent and colorimetric mode. The linear regression equations were Y = −0.3546X + 1.022 (R2 = 0.9979) and Y = 0.2884X + 0.991 (R2 = 0.9989).

Schematic illustration of the design of the mAb for sensitive and rapid immunosensor of BA. a) Preparation of mAb. b) Schematic diagram of the construction of dual-modular fluorometric and colorimetric immunosensor for BA detection.
The cross-reactivity (CR, %) with other small toxins using ic-ELISA.
Recoveries of spiked samples by dual-modular immunosensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Dual-modular immunosensor for bongkrekic acid detection using specific monoclonal antibody

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, South China Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bongkrekic acid (BA) is a mitochondrial toxin that causes high mortality but is often mistakenly categorized as other food poisonings. The immunoassay of BA is still challenging since the specific antibody is unavailable. In this work, a monoclonal antibody specific to BA was first generated and a dual-modular immunosensor for on-site and laboratory detection was established. The antibody showed good affinity (Kd=0.33 μM) and sensitivity (IC50 =17.9 ng/mL in ELISA) with negligible cross-reactivity with common mycotoxins. In dual-modular conditions, fluorescence assay (FA) was conducted based on the inner filter effect of carbon dots (CDs) and oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), while the colorimetric assay (CA) was conducted using TMB2+-mediated rapid surface etching of gold nanostars (Au NSs). The proposed immunosensor showed good sensitivity and reproducibility to BA in food samples, with a limit of detection lower than 10 ng/mL and recovery ranging from 80.0% to 103.6%, which was in good consistence with that of standard LC-MS/MS. Overall, the proposed immunosensor is an ideal tool for screening BA contaminants in food with good sensitivity and high effectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Bongkrekic acid (BA), a mitochondrial toxin, was first discovered in 1930 by the Dutch scholars Mertens and Vanveen [10,26]. Since 1975, consumption of infected tempe bongkrek has resulted in nearly 3000 cases of BA toxicity, including at least 150 deaths [1]. Lately, a family in Jixi, Heilongjiang province, China, passed away after eating corn flour contaminated with BA. However, it was initially incorrectly considered a food poisoning incident caused by aflatoxin [38,43]. After that, the hazards of BA have been gradually emphasized. BA is secreted by the pathogenic variant of Burkholderia gladioli pv. cocovenenans, which easily contaminates fermented grain products, tremella, and agaric. It mainly causes poisoning by acting on the respiratory chain system and blocking the exchange of adenosine diphosphate and adenosine triphosphate between the inner membrane of mitochondria [10]. The incubation period of BA in humans is 1–10 h, with an oral half-lethal dose of 3.16 mg/kg. Moreover, the mortality rate of BA is more than 40%, which is much higher than that of other common bacterial food poisoning [1,41]. Consequently, monitoring BA is crucial for food security and human health.

Previously, instrumental methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry were the primary analytical methods for BA detection [14,19]. Nevertheless, these methods come with the disadvantages of requiring expensive reagents, specialized equipment, and technical personnel. Recently, Zhang et al. Zhang et al., ($year$) [41] reported a UV-Vis and smartphone APP dual-mode detection platform for BA based on the unique optical properties of gold nanoparticles and the electrostatic interaction between gold nanoparticles and BA, with a limit of detection down to 6.0 ng/mL. However, this method has some limitations of false positives and low specificity. In this regard, specific recognition of antigens and antibodies immunoassay could be a potential method for monitoring BA due to its high sensitivity, specificity, and economy [23,36]. Colorimetric assay (CA) has inherent advantages in terms of convenience and visual detection, but its low sensitivity has limited its application in single modal readout. Indeed, noble metal nanostructures have been widely investigated in recent years for developing colorimetric sensors with the naked eye without using complex equipment [17,29]. Fluorescence assay (FA) relying mainly on internal rate effect exhibits higher sensitivity and accuracy than traditional colorimetric sensors [27,32]. As for FA, the fluorescent probes are closely related to the method’s sensitivity. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs) have also attracted much attention in the last decade due to their excellent properties, such as high photoluminescence quantum yields, abundant raw materials on the earth, low production costs, eco-friendly preparation, making them an ideal fluorescent probe for FA ( [40]). Recently, a dual-split-type immunosensor has quietly entered the market to meet the precision and sensitivity requirements of quantitative analysis, with the benefits of colorimetry or fluorimetry and the ability to prevent oscillations by using dual-channel signals as self-control [3,31,4].

To our knowledge, the anti-BA antibody obtained in this work is the first. Typically, a monoclonal antibody (mAb) against BA with good sensitivity and specificity has been prepared by optimizing the immunogen and selecting the hybridomas. Based on this, a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-modular immunosensor based on red CDs and gold nanostars (Au NSs) was established for on-site and laboratory detection of BA. In Scheme 1, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was oxidized by horseradish peroxidase to oxTMB, which overlapped well with the emission fluorescence spectrum of CDs (650 nm). The regularity of fluorescence quenching is induced by triggering the internal rate effect. Meanwhile, the released oxidation product of oxTMB etched the Au NSs to form the colored product for visual detection. In this study, the developed immunosensor showed excellent detection performance for BA by coupling fluorescence and visual signals simultaneously, which paves the way for sensitive and rapid detection on-site.

BA standards were provided by ANPEL Laboratory Technologies Inc (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin, lactoferrin (LF), and ovalbumin (OVA) were obtained from Shanghai yuan ye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). TMB, N-Hydroxy succinimide, 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride and sodium citrate were purchased from Aladdin were provided by Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, and urea were from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). Chloroauric acid and silver nitrate were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Polyethylene glycol, ascorbic acid, and N, N-dimethylformamide were Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). A secondary antibody (Goat anti-mouse IgG, HRP conjugated) and protein G resin were purchased by TransGen Biotech (Beijing, China). Freund’s adjuvant complete, and Freund’s adjuvant incomplete were procured from Sigma Corporation (St. Louis, USA). Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was purchased from MYM Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Balb/c female mice were purchased from Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. and raised at the Animal Experiment Centre of South China Agriculture University (Animal Experiment Ethical Approval Number: 2021B050). All reagents were of analytical grade, and all solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm, 25 °C) from the Millipore Milli-Q system. The batch number of all the materials was in Supporting Material (Table S1).

The morphology of Au NSs and CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Thermo Fisher, America). The structure and elements of CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker-AXS, Germany), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, America). The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were acquired using a SpectraMax i3 microplate reader (Molecular Devices, USA). The enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) plates were washed in a Wellwash™ microplate washer (Thermo Fisher, USA). High-performance liquid chromatography analysis was performed using a Waters-2695 liquid chromatograph (Milford, America).

A hapten (1.0 mM), N-Hydroxy succinimide (1.5 mM), and 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (1.5 mM) were mixed in dimethylformamide (1.0 mL) and agitated for 4 h at room temperature to convert the carboxylic acid group of hapten into an active ester. The mixture was then added to 1.0 mL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 8.0, 0.01 M, 1.0 mL) containing carrier protein (10.0 mg/mL LF or OVA) and stirred at 4 °C overnight. The conjugation mixture (immunogen or coating antigen) was dialyzed in PBS (pH 8.0, 0.01 M, 5.0 L) for 3 days before being stored at − 20 °C.

Preparing good-quality antibodies is the primary task for establishing an excellent detection method [5]. The immunization method was consistent with those reported in the literature. Six balb/c female mice (8–9 weeks of age) were immunized with the immunogen (BA-LF). Initially, an immunogen (1.0 mg/mL) emulsified with complete Freund's adjuvant (0.1 mL) was subcutaneously injected into the hackles and enterocoelia of mice, followed by three booster injections every three weeks using the same immunizing dose with incomplete Freund's adjuvant. The mouse with the best titer was selected as the hybridoma cell donor for the spleen. Polyethylene glycol-induced spleen cells fuse with SP2/0 myeloma cells to form hybridoma cells. Later, the hybridoma cells secreting specific antibodies of BA were subcloned five times using limited dilution. The best antibody-producing clone was amplified and selected to produce ascetic antibodies (Scheme 1a). Finally, the concentration of IC50 of the curve was used to calculate the cross-reactivity (CR, %) of the assay, and the CR formula was as follows: CR (%) = IC50 (BA)/ IC50 (cross-reactive compounds) × 100.

CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal preparation [6,40]. Approximately 1 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea were dissolved in a 10 mL N, N-dimethylformamide solution, agitated for 10 min, transferred into a 30 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave, and then hydrothermally treated at 160 °C in an oven for 6 h. After the reaction solvent was cooled to room temperature, a dark red solution was acquired. Then, the obtained solution filtered by a 0.22 µm membrane was mixed with 20 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (50 mg/mL) and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to collect the sediment. Later, the sediment was dispersed in ultra-pure water to centrifuge again. Finally, the sediment was redispersed in ultrapure water to achieve the red-light emitting CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the starting Au NSs
ce:section.ce:para: Au NSs were prepared according to the previously published methods with minor modifications [22,37]. Approximately 10 mL of chloroauric acid (10 mM) and 1 mL of sodium citrate (40 mM) were mixed with vigorous shaking at 100 °C for 5 min. After one minute, the color of the mixture swiftly transformed from yellow to red wine, signifying the development of gold seeds. Then, at 30 °C, 50 mL of chloroauric acid (1.0 mM) was vigorously mixed with 5 mL of gold seeds, 0.5 mL of hydrochloric acid (1.0 M), 2.0 mL of silver nitrate (5.0 mM), and 1 mL of ascorbic acid (100 mM). Au NSs were formed when the solution turned a dark blue color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of dual-modular immunosensor for BA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The only difference between the procedure of the developed immunosensor and the conventional ELISA is in the color-display step: in this proposed method, a mixture solution consisting of 10% H2SO4 and Au NSs is added to obtain various colors, whereas, in conventional ELISA, 10% H2SO4 is required. Additionally, the method was combined with fluorometry to achieve exceptional accuracy and sensitivity (Scheme 1b). Firstly, a 96-microwell plate was coated with 100 μL of BA-OVA (1 μg/mL) and incubated for a whole night at 37 °C. The plate was then rinsed twice with 0.1 M PBST( PBS containing 0.5% Tween 20), blocked with 3% bovine albumin in 0.1 M PBS (200 μL/well) at 37 °C for 2 h, and dried at 37 °C for 30 min. Later, 50 μL of BA standards (at various concentrations in PBS) was added to each well, followed by adding 50 μL of diluted anti-BA antibody in PBS. When the mixture was incubated for 40 min at 37 °C, the wells were washed with PBST five times. Subsequently, 100 μL of secondary antibody (1:5000 in PBST) was injected and incubated for 30 min, and the plate was rewashed five times to remove the unconjugated solution. Then, a TMB liquid substrate (200 μL) was added to the plate and incubated for 12 min at 37 °C. Finally, 100 μL of the above mixture was transferred into the black plate, which was precoated with 50 μL of CDs and sodium hydroxide (50.0 mg/mL), and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by a spectrofluorometer between the scanning range of 570–620 nm with an excitation wavelength at 540 nm. For CA, 40 μL of H2SO4 (10%, v/v) was added to 100 μL of the residual mixture. Then, 50 μL of CTAB (0.1 M) and 50 μL of Au NSs were injected one after another. After being reacted for 20 min at room temperature, the color and the absorption spectra (from 400 to 800 nm) were recorded using Xiaomi 10 s and a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The dual-modular immunosensor calibration curves were fitted by plotting the fluorescence intensity of CDs or the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) blue-shifted value of AuNSs (∆λ, ∆λ = λmax – λ, λmax represents wavelength value at no BA, and λ represents wavelength value at the maximum BA concentration) against the logarithm of the BA concentration. The assay’s detection limit (LOD) was determined as IC10 with the lower and upper limits of quantitative concentration as IC20-IC80, respectively.

The performance of dual modular immunoassay in real samples was evaluated by spiking recovery and liquid chromatograph mass spectrometer/mass spectrometer (LC-MS/MS) analyses. Realamples (rice flour, agaric, tremella) were provided by Guangzhou Food Inspection Institute (Guangzhou, China), which were negative for BA. About 100, 200, and 400 ng/g of BA were added to the samples as the positive control. The negative control of a blank sample was prepared similarly and evaluated, and all the extractions were diluted 20-fold to eliminate the matrix effect.

The UV–vis spectra of the final binding and carrier protein were collected to verify the successful conjugation of the hapten to the carrier protein. Fig. S1 shows the characteristic absorbance peak of BA at 287 nm, whereas OVA and LF showed a maximum absorbance peak at about 220 nm. BA-OVA and BA-LF displayed a specific absorbance peak at 230 nm, reflecting the properties of a carrier protein and a hypsochromic shift with a distinct absorbance compared with BA. Thus, the UV–vis spectra were recorded to confirm the successful binding of haptens to a carrier protein.

BA (molecular weight of 486 Da) is a small molecule toxin without immunogenicity and must be coupled to a carrier protein to form a complete antigen. In this study, the activated ester method was used to conjugate BA with LF to obtain an immunogen and with OVA to obtain a coating antigen [5,24]. After immunizing the mice (Fig. S2) and five rounds of subcloning, one hybridoma cell line (1B9) secreting mAb against BA was obtained stably. After being purified by protein G resin, the mAb showed good purity ( Fig. 1 a), and its isotype was recognized as IgG1 (Fig. S3). Furtherly, CR was used to evaluate the specificity of the developed mAb. Negligible CR was observed for several commonly used toxins ( Table 1), indicating that the mAb was feasible for analytical detection. Under the optimal ELISA condition, the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) was 17.9 ng/mL (Fig. 1 b). The isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) experiments were conducted to confirm the affinity between the mAb (B9) and BA [9,21]. The dissociation constant by ITC was 0.33 μM (Fig. 1 c-d), showing good binding affinity of the obtained antibody.

As a fluorescent probe for FA, the reaction product was characterized by TEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and XRD to prove the successful synthesis of CDs. The TEM results of CDs are shown in Fig. 2 a, in which as-synthesized CDs showed a uniform spherical shape and displayed a uniform size. The HRTEM image in the inset image of Fig. 2a1 reveals the high crystallinity of CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. The particle size of the CDs was about 2.0–4.5 nm with an average diameter of 3.5 nm (Fig. 2 a
2 ). The XRD pattern of CDs showed a prominent peak centered at 2θ = 26.7°(Fig. 2 b), corresponding to the (002)-diffraction peak of the graphite structure of CDs [40]. The optical performances of the CDs were investigated using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The typical absorption and emission spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. 2 c. A robust red emission spectrum was observed under Ex = 540 nm. The inset provided a more vivid color change of CDs under ultraviolet light. Moreover, the zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime of CDs with TMB were examined to study the quenching mechanism of the developed FA. As shown in Fig. S4, both CDs and oxTMB were negatively charged, suggesting that there was a negligible effect of electrostatic attraction. The results followed the zeta potential of CDs (−10.4 mV) [16]. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was 3.41 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime of CDs with oxTMB was 3.37 ns, indicating that the quenching effect of CDs was due to the internal rate effect rather than fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Fig. 2 d) [27]. The functional groups of CDs were determined using Fourier transform infrared spectrometer and XPS. The vibration absorption peak between 3100 and 3500 cm−1 indicated the presence of hydrophilic groups in CDs, mainly corresponding to the tensile vibration of O-H or N-H (Fig. 2 e) [7,35]. The weak bands located at 2800 − 3000 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C−H [34,42]. The broad peak at 1600 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C-C/C= [13,40]. Moreover, the stretching vibrations of C-N = (1427 cm−1), CO (1350 cm−1), and C-O-C (1168 cm−1) bonds were observed for each sample, indicating that CDs contain ether or phenol structure [11], and the bending vibration of C and H on the benzene ring might act as the source of the absorption peak at 908 cm−1 [33]. Furthermore, XPS was performed to study the composition and chemical state of CDs (Fig. 2 f). The full XPS spectrum of CDs exhibited seven typical peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. When the CDs were treated with NaOH, the spectra of the Na element (Na 1 s, Na 2 s, Na 2p) appeared [39]. As shown in Fig. 2 g-i, the chemical bond conjugations of C, N, and O in CDs were reflected in the high-resolution XPS spectra. Upon deconvolution, the C1s spectrum displayed four peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.4 eV (Fig. 2 g), corresponding to the functional groups of CC/C-C, C-N/C-O, and OC-O, respectively [40,7]. As for N1s (Fig. 2 h), the peaks at 398.6 eV and 399.9 eV were attributed to pyridinic CN–C and pyrrolic N, respectively [20,28]. The O1s XPS spectrum peaks (Fig. 2 i) at 531.2 eV, 532.5 eV, and 536.0 eV were attributed to CO, OC-O, and water [8,15]. These observations confirmed that CDs have a conjugation core in pyridine and pyrrolic forms, which exhibited efficient long-wavelength fluorescence emission performance.

The average size of Au NSs made using the seed-mediated method was about 70 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the prepared Au NSs were covered with horns resembling stars. Previous studies [12,22,37] have reported that with the co-existence of H2O2, TMB, and CTAB in the solution, TMB2+ can etch Au NSs easily, especially take place through an electrochemical reaction at the interface, beginning with the rounded tips of Au NSs(Fig. 3 b), followed by spherical nanostructures (Fig. 3 c). Furthermore, the energy dispersive spectrometer and element mapping showed that there was a significant amount of Au and a little Ag before etching. After etching, the increasing ratio of Au/Ag further demonstrates that Au is the primary material used for etching (Fig. S5). The detailed mechanism is mentioned in Eq. (1):
(1) TMB(II) + 2Au = TMB + 2Au(I)

Au NSs can be oxidized by TMB2+ with the assistance of CTAB, resulting in a blue shift of the longitudinal plasmon bands at room temperature (Fig. 3 d), in which the surrounding horns are dissolved in the solution. Different aspect ratios of the Au NSs exhibited multicolor due to the LSPR effect. Moreover, the longitudinal plasmon band at 690 nm showed a blue shift (Fig. 3 e), shifting from 690 to 550 nm. Additionally, the three typical morphologies of Au NSs solutions were blue, purple, and pink, respectively (the inset of Fig. 3 e).

As for FA, the fluorescent intensity of CDs was susceptibly sensitive to the pH value [25,33]. The fluorescent intensity of CDs in the alkaline solution (7 <pH<13) was significantly greater than that of the acidic solution (2 <pH<7) (Fig. S6a). The optimal pH condition for HRP activity was 3.8, in which the fluorescent intensity of CDs was weak [6]. Thus, CDs with different pH buffers were introduced into the reacting system after oxTMB was generated. In the optimization of pH, oxTMB at a pH value of 11 exhibited the best efficiency in quenching the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S6b). Additionally, the dilution times of CDs are another key correlated with the fluorescence intensity. The as-prepared CDs were first used in the reaction. The max fluorescent intensity was reduced by only 39.4% ( Fig. 4 a). After being diluted 4 times, the quenching efficiency reached 94.1% (Fig. 4 b). As the CDs were diluted 16 times, the quenching effect was evident. However, the unsmooth curves were no longer fit for quantitative analysis (Fig. 4 c). Therefore, CDs diluted 4 times were used in the following detection.

The colorimetric assay relied on the reaction between TMB2+ and Au NSs, and TMB2+ was produced in the HRP-H2O2-TMB system ([12]; Ma et al., 2017). Hence, the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB were first evaluated. The LSPR blue shift tended to stabilize at 4.0 mM H2O2 (Figs. 4 d) and 2.0 mM TMB with the increase in H2O2/TMB concentration (Fig. 4 e). Thus, the optimum concentrations of H2O2 and TMB were 4.0 mM and 2.0 mM, respectively. It is noteworthy that the etching can only be accomplished with the aid of CTAB since the direct reaction between Au NSs and TMB2+ was thermodynamically unsatisfactory due to the redox potential of TMB2+/TMB, i.e., 0.74 V (vs. NHE) [2,18], which was significantly lower than Au+/Au (1.51 V (vs. NHE)). AuBr2 --CTA+/Au has a redox potential of less than 0.2 V (vs. NHE) [30]. Thus, the CTAB concentration was optimized. As shown in Fig. 4 f, the LSPR blue shift steadily increased with the increase of CTAB concentration and approached a plateau at 0.1 M. Therefore, 0.1 M CTAB was employed in this study. Other optimizations can be found in the supplementary materials(Fig. S7–8).

Under the optimum condition, a dual-modular immunosensor with fluorometric and colorimetric assay was developed for BA detection. In the range from 0 to 100 ng/mL, a relationship was studied between fluorescence intensity and ∆λ. Significantly, both the fluorescence intensity and the LSPR blue-shifted value have a sigmoidal fitting vs the concentrations of BA ( Fig. 5 a-b). At the low concentration of BA, more oxTMB will be generated, which could quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 620 nm, forming a single signal. Surprisingly, the color of the solution changed from purple to blue (Fig. S9), which was easily recognized by the naked eye. Simultaneously, more TMB2+ will be converged on AuNSs, leading shorten of it by the redox reaction. Then we can record the LSPR peak position of AuNSs, getting another signal, with the color changed from blue to purple, and pink (Fig. 5 c). The limit of BA in tremella specified in the National Food Safety Standard of China GB7096 is 0.25 mg/kg, and other countries have not seen limit requirements. Compared with traditional instrument methods, the LOD for fluorometric and colorimetric detections are calculated to be 5.7 ng/mL and 8.4 ng/mL, respectively. Hence, the proposed method can also reach the ng level while the instrument method has better sensitivity, which is far higher than the detection limit of this method. More importantly, the developed immunosensor has obvious advantages in on-site detection compared with the current instrumental method (Table S2). The exactitude is a necessary criterion for immunoassay, so the recoveries of BA from a spiked sample of the developed dual-modular immunosensor were studied. As shown in Fig. S10a, after the extraction, the solution was diluted with 0.01 M PBS to eliminate the matrix effect of the immunosensor. For all the test samples, a dilution of 10 was enough to remove the matrix effect (Fig. S10b -d). Under the same conditions, the recovery test was performed, and the results are shown in Table 2. Satisfactory recoveries ranging from 80.0% to 103.6% and relative standard deviation (RSD) from 1.2% to 13.0% (n = 3) were obtained for three spiked levels of BA (100, 200, and 400 ng/g). These findings indicated the high reproducibility of the dual-modular immunoassay. Additionally, to confirm the reliability of the dual-modal immunosensor, 60 blind samples were also measured and compared with the LC-MS/MS results (Table S3). The details of the LC−MS/MS method can be found in Supporting Information (Table S4). The blind sample data from the FA(R2 = 0.9979) and CA (R2 = 0.9989)were fitted with LC-MS-MS to validate the strong correlation further (Fig. 5 d). Compared with traditional single modality assay, the developed dual-modular immunosensor displayed the following advantage. First, the research integrated fluorescent and colorimetric modalities into the BA detection system, from which the analysis results not only can be quantitatively judged based on the obvious colorimetric change via naked-eye observation but also can be accurately measured by fluorescence signal with significantly enhanced sensitivity. Second, thanks to different signal response pathways, the two signal routes are independent in the detection of BA. Ultimately, the marriage of colorimetric and “turn off” fluorescence immunosensor showed excellent detection performance for BA due to their independent response and mutual confirmation.

In summary, a cell line capable of secreting high-quality mAb (B9) against BA was selected first. Then, a representative dual-modular immunoassay based on mAb was constructed for BA detection, with the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the LSPR blue shift of Au NSs. The ingenious combination of TMB2+ etching Au NSs and CDs extinction endowed the assay with preferable abilities, including multimode signal output and high sensitivity. The established dual-modular immunoassay showed a LOD of 5.7 ng/mL (FA) and 8.4 ng/mL (CA) for BA, which is far higher than the limit requirement of BA. The recoveries of the sample spiked with BA ranging from 80.0% to 103.6%, and the RSD ranged from 1.2% to 13.0%. Compared with traditional immunoassays, the designed dual-modular immunoassay showed a good correlation with LC-MS/MS, offering a more accurate and reliable result benefiting from different mechanisms and independent signal transduction.

Bongkrekic acid (BA) is a mitochondrial toxin secreted by the pathogenic variant of Burkholderia gladioli pv. cocovenenans (BGC), which can easily contaminate fermented grain products, tremella, and agaric, resulting in a fatality rate higher than 50%. However, the most used approaches for BA detection are instrumental methods, rapid and sensitive methods for BA detection are still a challenge work. In this work, we established a monoclonal antibody (mAb) with good selectivity to BA for the first time. Subsequently, we have established a dual-modular immunosensor for on-site and laboratory of BA and food contaminated with BA.

Xue-Ming Cao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data analysis, Writing – original draft. Li-Hua Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation. Hong-zhi Liang: Methodology, Validation. Jia-Dong Li: Software guidance, Data analysis. Zi-Jian Chen: Software guidance, Data analysis. Lin Luo: Validation, Methodology. Yi-Na Lu: Methodology. Yu-Xin Zhong: Software, Resources. Yu-Dong Shen: Supervision, Investigation. Hong-Tao Lei: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Hong Wang: Writing – review & editing. Zhen-Lin Xu: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (32061160473), the Science and Technology Project of Dengta Laboratory, China (DT20220033), and the Science and Technology Project of Shantou Custom, China (2022STK001).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2023.131634.
